critique.summary: A tech hobbyist named Alisher Khojayev created a shoulder-mounted, 3D-printed guided missile prototype costing $96 in parts. The device includes a launcher, missile, and optional camera tracking system, using off-the-shelf electronics and 3D-printed components, but it is essentially a model rocket with guidance and not a functional weapon.
mainPoints: The missile system prototype is made mostly from 3D-printed parts and inexpensive electronics like ESP32 microprocessors and sensors.
The launcher and missile communicate via Wi-Fi for telemetry and guidance calculations.
The prototype costs about $96, significantly cheaper than military-grade MANPADS like the Stinger missile, which costs hundreds of thousands of dollars.
The creator and the article emphasize that this is a tech demonstration or toy, not a practical or effective weapon.
There are significant practical limitations such as low engine power, material durability, and vulnerability to electronic warfare in contested environments.
The project and documentation are publicly available, but Tom's Hardware does not endorse making weapons at home.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately reports on the hobbyist's project and the technical details of the prototype based on the available video and documentation. The cost comparison to military-grade missiles is factual and highlights the vast difference in capability and reliability. The article responsibly clarifies that the device is not a functional weapon but a guided model rocket prototype. Comments from experts and hobbyists included in the article further support the limitations and impracticality of the device as a weapon. There is no evidence that the prototype poses a real threat or that it can function as a military-grade missile.
considerations: The article does not deeply explore legal implications or regulations regarding manufacturing or possessing guided missile technology, which can be significant.
Potential safety risks or misuse scenarios of such hobbyist projects are not extensively discussed.
The durability and reliability of 3D-printed parts under operational stresses are only briefly mentioned in comments, not analyzed in detail.
The article does not address countermeasures or electronic warfare challenges that would render Wi-Fi and GPS guidance ineffective in real combat.
Ethical considerations about publishing how-to information for potentially dangerous technology are touched on in comments but not explored in the main article.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on a hobbyist's shoulder-mounted guided missile prototype, with comments debating its technical feasibility, legal implications, and broader concerns about arms control and emerging DIY weapon technologies. Some commenters express skepticism about the device's capabilities, while others highlight the challenges of regulating such homemade weapons.

comments.author: Hasz
comments.text:
Regardless of whether this actually works (I have my doubts, but also understand it might be difficult to get range time on a device like this :)), it exposes a fundamental issue with arms control today.

Small firearms are hundreds of years old. Drones have been commercially available for many years and are easily modifiable into something that is 80% as good as what is currently being fielded in Ukraine.

It is not technically feasible to restrict someone from assembling basic, non-firearm-specific components to build a firearm. In the US, there is an increasing effort at the state level to serialize, restrict, and document individual firearm parts. However, an 80% good barrel can be fabricated at home, a 100% as good receiver can be printed on any recent 3D printer, and the rest of the parts (bolt, trigger assembly, etc) can be designed around easy home fabrication (see FGC-9). There is no practical way to trace, regulate, or stop behavior.

It isn't possible to restrict someone from building a capable drone either. The firmware is opensource, the parts can be ordered from almost any marketplace, and an energetic payload can easily be made by any amateur chemist from chemicals in any hardware or camping store. EW is often touted as a solution, but is frequently beaten by tethered drones. Cheap COTS IMUs are getting good enough to provide surprisingly accurate short-term INS, to say nothing of autonomous systems that need no external input past initial targeting.

I personally think this is a far bigger risk than most countries realize, largely because they are 10-15 years behind the technology. I believe this will force most governments into spending an order of magnitude more to defend their institutions at every level, not just core government security.

At least in the US, these threat vectors will absolutely be used to justify intrusions into civil liberties, but no amount of infringement will be able to even partially mitigate these threats. I think this should start to play out over the next 5-10 years.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a well-reasoned and informed perspective on the limitations of current arms control in the face of DIY weapons and drone technology, aligning with known challenges in regulation.

comments.author: the__alchemist
comments.text:
I will be a negative yancy, and regurgitate things from the previous thread in combination with my pattern-matching brain and experience with making UAS firmware/hardware etc.

Cool project, but this is the 1% of the work that's required to get an initial platform in place. It cannot intercept an airborne target, and it will take the rest of the 99% of the work on testing, refining guidance/propulsion/sensors etc, finding and fixing errors, finding and fixing incorrect assumptions that will lead to re-building various subsystems etc.

Another way of phrasing it is that this is a cargo cult MANPADS.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately critiques the prototype's early stage and the significant work still needed to create a functional guided missile system, reflecting realistic technical challenges.

comments.author: CryptoBanker
comments.text:
In the two test launches shown in the video, the "missile" doesn't fly straight nor does it demonstrate ability to be "guided" by the launcher towards any particular target.

It's also incredibly slow. There are children's rocket kits that fly significantly faster than this.
comments.analysis: The observation about the missile's poor flight performance and lack of guidance is consistent with the video evidence and technical expectations for such a prototype.

comments.author: briandw
comments.text: I think this violates ITAR. You aren't allowed put a guidance system on a rocket. And even if you were allowed to do it for your own fun/education, you certainly aren't allowed to provide instructions to foreign entities about how it do it.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly raises legal concerns regarding ITAR regulations, which restrict the export and dissemination of missile guidance technology.

comments.author: sidewndr46
comments.text:
Here's a link to the actual video:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DDO2EvXyncE

This appears to be flight stabilized and guided via direct command coming from the launcher. It is not an autonomous guided missile.
comments.analysis: The clarification about the missile being remotely guided rather than autonomous aligns with the technical description and video evidence.

comments.author: swiftcoder
comments.text: When everyone started working on 3D-printed guns, I was sitting here thinking that if it comes to actual revolution, one is going to need anti-tank/anti-air a whole lot more than (relatively easy to acquire) small arms... Nice to see movement on this front
comments.analysis: This comment reflects a personal viewpoint emphasizing the strategic importance of heavier weapons, though it is speculative and less grounded in technical detail.

comments.author: cucumber3732842
comments.text:
I think with the proliferation and effectiveness of countermeasures passive target acquisition and first shot accuracy with traditional ballistic methods might be a better place to focus but I understand that's very hard to do nonprofessionally as an individual thanks to the rules and laws.

On the other hand, there is a lot to be said for making them blow their $1k active countermeasures on your $500 missiles before sending a real one in to finish the job. Heck, even just forcing your adversary to treat every sky like it's hostile is worth a lot.

Both approaches are clearly worthy of development.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a nuanced tactical perspective on missile effectiveness and countermeasure strategies, though it is more speculative about practical implementation.

comments.author: robertlagrant
comments.text:
> Despite the tech-cool factor of the project, Tom's Hardware does not condone making your own weapons system at home.

Not that this matters for the topic, but I don't see why people have started saying "weapons system" instead of "weapon".
comments.analysis: This comment adds little substantive value to the discussion and focuses on semantics rather than technical or legal aspects.

comments.author: bparsons
comments.text: We might want to prepare ourselves for the fact that the Strait of Hormuz might not be reopened to US traffic any time in the near future.
comments.analysis: This geopolitical speculation is tangential and not supported by evidence within the context of the missile prototype discussion.

comments.author: throw0101d
comments.text:
On the defensive side, see perhaps this phased array radar system with an 20km range:

* https://github.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR
comments.analysis: This comment contributes a relevant technical resource but does not directly address the missile prototype's capabilities or implications.

comments.author: yorwba
